narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 3.00, an LSAT score of 24, lower-middle family income, and your plan to attend full-time, along with your school deciles from Year 1 and Year 3 both at the 1st decile—meaning your early grades placed you at the bottom of your class compared to peers. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on patterns from thousands of similar students, estimated a 59.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam, tipping the scales just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven assessment to help the school select applicants most likely to thrive and pass on their first try. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking in the bottom 1st percentile—far below the typical 5th or 6th decile—suggesting the model saw your later academic performance as a strong signal of challenges ahead on the rigorous bar exam. Your LSAT score of 24, well under the average of about 37, was the next biggest driver toward predicting failure, as lower scores like yours have historically correlated with tougher bar outcomes in the dataset. On a more positive note, your lower-middle family income—below the dataset's average bracket—actually worked in your favor, nudging the prediction toward success, perhaps reflecting resilience or other strengths associated with similar backgrounds. Compared to typical students who pass, where stronger deciles and LSATs dominate, your profile aligns more closely with those who struggle, even as a full-time student like over 90% of applicants. This isn't set in stone; many in your position, including Hispanic men from similar incomes, have succeeded by targeting improvements. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the average, seeking academic advising to elevate your class rankings in any remaining coursework, and exploring programs with strong bar prep support tailored to first-generation or underrepresented students like yourself. Building a narrative around your unique strengths—perhaps through recommendation letters highlighting your determination—could also strengthen future applications. With focused steps, you can shift these odds and pursue your legal ambitions.
